perturbation.original_text: The notices and other expressions of intent for the purpose of this Agreement shall be deemed received: when delivered by express delivery or personal delivery, at the actual time of receipt; when delivered by mail, at the actual time of receipt or 72 hours after mailing (whichever is earlier).
perturbation.changed_text: The notices and other expressions of intent for the purpose of this Agreement shall be deemed received: when delivered by express delivery or personal delivery, at the actual time of receipt; when delivered by mail, 12 hours after mailing.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the time for deemed receipt of mail to 12 hours is unreasonable and conflicts with standard international mailing practices and expectations of reasonable notice.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General principles of contract law - Reasonable Notice
perturbation.law_citation: General principles of contract law - Reasonable Notice
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification creates a situation where receipt of notices by mail is presumed within an unreasonably short timeframe (12 hours). This contradicts the general principle of 'reasonable notice' in contract law. While Taiwan law governs, the principle of providing adequate time for notice to be received and acted upon is a universally recognized tenet of contract enforceability. The original text was 72 hours, which is more accepted.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: notice | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you notice Notice is the constitutional requirement that a party being brought into court be informed of the controversy . Due Process clauses of the United States Constitution prohibit courts from hearing a case that could adversely affect a party's interest unless that party has been given proper notice. To satisfy this notice requirement, notice must be reasonably calculated, under the circumstances, to inform all interested parties that a lawsuit is pending and that it could adversely affect their interests. The degree of required notice varies depending on what type of jurisdiction a court intends to exercise. To exercise in personam jurisdiction, in-hand service of process is usually required. For in rem actions, which can affect the interests of anyone in the world, the plaintiff usually must inform all known parties of interest by a reasonably reliable means, and may then inform the rest of the world by "notice by publication" - purchasing a notice in a local newspaper multiple times over a period of several weeks. In tricky cases, courts work with plaintiffs to identify the best way of meeting notice requirements. In some unusual circumstances, a court may find traditional methods of notice are not satisfactory for a particular party. Notice is not a mere formality, as the Supreme Court recently discussed in Jones v. Flowers . In Jones v. Flowers , a state seized and sold property for unpaid property taxes. A certified letter meant to notify the owner of the impending sale was returned to the state as "unclaimed." Afterwards, the state made no additional attempt to contact the owner directly. The Court rejected the state's notice as insufficient, emphasizing that notice “must be such as one desirous of actually informing the absentee might reasonably adopt to accomplish it." Because of the constitutional importance of ensuring proper notice, courts will not forgive notice achieved through improper means even where a party actually learns of the controversy. [Last reviewed in October of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex CIVICS the Constitution THE LEGAL PROCESS wex articles civil procedure constitutional law courts and procedure individual rights Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text shortens the notification period to 12 hours after mailing, which is an unreasonable timeframe. The Perturbation Explanation states that this conflicts with standard international mailing practices and expectations of reasonable notice. The Law Explanation states that it contradicts the principle of reasonable notice. Scraped Law Snippet 1 emphasizes the constitutional importance of ensuring proper notice, stating that notice "must be such as one desirous of actually informing the absentee might reasonably adopt to accomplish it." A 12-hour window does not satisfy this requirement, as it is not a reasonably reliable means of ensuring the intended party receives notice, thus creating an Inconsistency Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties shall not make an announcement to the public without the consent of the Parties regarding the execution and content of this Agreement and information in connection with the performance of this Agreement, which includes, but is not limited to the disclosure of material information, pursuant to the laws and the content thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties shall not make an announcement to the public regarding the execution and content of this Agreement and information in connection with the performance of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause "pursuant to the laws and the content thereof" effectively prohibits mandatory disclosure of the agreement and material information, which violates securities laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Taiwan Securities and Exchange Act, Article 36
perturbation.law_citation: Taiwan Securities and Exchange Act, Article 36
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 36 of the Taiwan Securities and Exchange Act requires listed companies to disclose material information to the public. By removing the phrase "pursuant to the laws and the content thereof", the modified clause attempts to restrict disclosures that may be legally mandated under Taiwan law, thus creating a legal contradiction. The original clause acknowledges the laws regarding such announcements, but the modification prohibits them outright.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: About the Law Library | Law Library of Congress | Research Centers | Library of Congress Top of page Notice: Starting June 24, upgrades to the Library's online catalog will temporarily impact the process for requesting Law materials in the Law Library Reading Room. View the Law Library's FAQ for information and instructions. Close Research Center Law Library of Congress About the Law Library Pause Legal Research Institute We offer a wide variety of legal research classes on topics of U.S. legal research, as well as foreign and comparative law. Learn more Rare Book Collections The Law Library's collection of rare books consists of approximately 90,000 volumes of books and bound manuscripts, many issued prior to 1801. Learn more Law Library Reading Room A staff of law librarians are available to help during your visit to the Law Library Reading Room. Learn more Digital Collections Many Law Library collections such as the U.S. Reports, Statutes at Large, Code of Federal Regulations, and Law Library Legal Reports are available online. Learn more Congress.gov Congress.gov is the official website for U.S. federal legislative information. Learn more The mission of the Law Library of Congress is to provide authoritative legal research, reference and instruction services, and access to an unrivaled collection of U.S., foreign, comparative, and international law. To accomplish this mission, the Law Library has assembled a staff of experienced foreign and U.S. trained legal specialists and law librarians, and has amassed the world's largest collection of law books and other legal resources from all countries, now comprising more than 2.9 million items. While research appointments are not required for the Law Library Reading Room, they are encouraged, especially when requesting materials held offsite. You can request an appointment here . perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the phrase "pursuant to the laws and the content thereof", which directly contradicts the requirement for disclosure of material information mandated by securities laws. The Perturbation Explanation states that this removal "effectively prohibits mandatory disclosure of the agreement and material information, which violates securities laws." The Law Explanation further clarifies this, stating the modified clause "attempts to restrict disclosures that may be legally mandated under Taiwan law". Without the original clause acknowledging legal mandates, the modified text creates a legal contradiction by attempting to override mandatory disclosures.
